title: Genoise sponge
subtitle: The classic French sponge, raised by eggs alone
excerpt: Genoise is the French sponge that uses no chemical leaveners and no fat beyond a thread of melted butter folded in at the end. The rise comes entirely from air beaten into whole eggs with sugar over heat. The result is a fine-crumbed, sturdy sponge that takes syrup well and holds its shape when layered. It is the base for many classic gateaux.
sourceNotes: The genoise (named for Genoa, though its modern form is primarily French) is documented in French patisserie texts from the early nineteenth century. It is distinguished from British-style sponge by warming the eggs and sugar together before whisking, which produces greater volume and a more stable foam. Auguste Escoffier's Le Guide Culinaire (1903) describes the method in detail. It remains the foundation of the French gateau tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap the cooled sponge and freeze for up to 1 month. Defrost at room temperature before splitting and filling.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake a day ahead and store wrapped at room temperature. The sponge benefits from resting before it is split and syruped.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 20 to 25 minutes until the sponge springs back and has pulled slightly from the sides of the tin

content.text: The critical step in genoise is warming the eggs and sugar together before whisking. Set the bowl over a pan of barely simmering water and stir until the mixture reaches about 40 degrees C and feels warm to the touch but not hot. This dissolves the sugar and loosens the egg proteins so they whisk to a much greater volume than cold eggs would. The resulting foam is pale, very thick, and three to four times the original volume.

Heat the oven to 175 degrees C fan. Grease a 20 cm round cake tin and line the base. In a large heatproof bowl, combine 3 large eggs, 100 g caster sugar, and 1 tsp vanilla. Set the bowl over a pan of barely simmering water, making sure the base does not touch the water. Whisk continuously until the mixture is warm to the touch and the sugar has dissolved, about 3 to 4 minutes. Remove from the heat and whisk with a hand mixer on high speed for 6 to 8 minutes until the mixture is very pale, thick, and has tripled in volume. A ribbon dropped from the beaters should hold on the surface for at least 5 seconds.

Sift 100 g plain flour over the whisked eggs in two additions, folding with a large metal spoon after each using wide, gentle strokes from the bottom of the bowl. Do not rush or fold more than necessary. Pour 30 g cooled melted butter down the side of the bowl and fold in with the final strokes of flour. Pour into the tin and bake for 20 to 25 minutes until the sponge springs back when pressed and has pulled slightly away from the sides. Leave in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a rack.

items.problem: The sponge is flat and dense
items.solution: The egg foam collapsed before baking, usually from folding too vigorously or from not whisking long enough. Whisk until genuinely thick and tripled in volume, and fold flour with a light hand using no more than 15 to 20 strokes per addition.
The melted butter was too warm when added and cooked the egg foam at the bottom. Cool the butter to room temperature before folding in.
